findings.description: Nested <main> landmark elements - ensure the document has at most one main landmark at top level
findings.evidence: <main> at line 27 contains another <main> at line 39. Only one <main> landmark is valid per document.

findings.description: The HTML adds internal anchor links in the table of contents that do not exist in the source PDF. The PDF simply lists sections I-IV as plain text without hyperlinks.
findings.evidence: HTML contains <a href="#history-background">, <a href="#march-29-2016-public-meeting-and-oral-comments-summary">, etc. These are navigational aids added by the converter, not fabricated external links.

findings.description: The HTML uses h1 for 'MEMORANDUM' and h2 for the four main sections (I-IV). The PDF uses 'MEMORANDUM' as a centered title and the sections as bold headings. This is a reasonable structural interpretation, though the document title might more naturally be the subject line rather than 'MEMORANDUM'.
findings.evidence:
HTML:

MEMORANDUM

followed by

for sections I-IV.

findings.description: The HTML contains nested <main> elements - there is a <main> in the body wrapper and another <main> element inside it, which is a structural duplication artifact but does not duplicate content.
findings.evidence: HTML has <body><main> wrapping everything, then inside the header section another <main> tag appears before section I.

findings.description: The section III heading text differs slightly between the PDF and HTML. The PDF uses 'Written Public Comment Summary' on page 4 while the table of contents on page 1 uses 'Written Public Comments Summary'. The HTML uses both forms in different locations, matching the PDF's inconsistency.
findings.evidence: PDF page 4: 'III. Written Public Comment Summary'; PDF page 1 TOC: 'III. Written Public Comments Summary'. HTML h2: 'III. Written Public Comment Summary'; HTML TOC: 'Written Public Comments Summary'. This mirrors the PDF's own inconsistency.
